AT.39740.conduct: Google systematically favoured its own comparison shopping service (Google Shopping) in its general search results pages, while demoting competing comparison shopping services.
AT.39740.finding: The Commission found that Google abused its dominant position on the market for general internet search by giving an illegal advantage to its own comparison shopping service. This constituted a breach of Article 102 TFEU.
AT.39740.court: Upheld by the General Court (T-612/17) in November 2021. Appeal (C-48/22 P) dismissed by the ECJ in September 2024, confirming the Commission's decision in full.
AT.39740.significance: Landmark self-preferencing case establishing that a dominant platform cannot systematically favour its own services in search results.

AT.40099.conduct: Google required Android device manufacturers to pre-install the Google Search app and Chrome browser as a condition for licensing the Play Store. Google also made payments to manufacturers and mobile network operators to exclusively pre-install Google Search.
AT.40099.finding: The Commission found three types of abuse: (1) illegal tying of Google Search and Chrome with the Play Store, (2) illegal payments for exclusive pre-installation of Google Search, (3) illegal obstruction of development of Android forks.
AT.40099.court: Largely upheld by the General Court (T-604/18) in September 2022, though the fine was slightly reduced to EUR 4.125 billion. Appeal pending (C-738/22 P).
AT.40099.significance: Largest-ever EU antitrust fine. Established that tying practices on mobile operating systems can constitute abuse of dominance.

AT.40411.conduct: Google imposed restrictive clauses in contracts with third-party websites (publishers) that used its search advertising intermediation service (AdSense for Search), preventing them from placing search ads from Google's competitors.
AT.40411.finding: The Commission found that Google abused its dominant position by imposing exclusivity and premium placement clauses that shut out competing providers of search advertising intermediation services.
AT.40411.court: Upheld by the General Court (T-334/19) in September 2024.
AT.40411.significance: Completed the Google antitrust trilogy. Addressed anticompetitive contractual restrictions in online advertising.

AT.39839.conduct: Telefonica and Portugal Telecom agreed not to compete with each other on the Iberian Peninsula after unwinding their Brazilian joint venture Vivo.
AT.39839.finding: Non-compete clause constituted a market-sharing agreement prohibited under Article 101 TFEU.
AT.39839.court: Upheld by the General Court (T-216/13) and ECJ (C-487/16 P).
AT.39839.significance: Clarified that non-compete clauses linked to transactions must be genuinely ancillary and proportionate.

AT.39861.conduct: Yen interest rate derivatives traders at multiple banks coordinated their trading activities and exchanged commercially sensitive information.
AT.39861.finding: Cartel involving manipulation of Yen LIBOR and Euroyen TIBOR benchmark interest rates through coordination between competing banks.
AT.39861.court: Multiple appeals, partly annulled for some parties.
AT.39861.significance: Major benchmark manipulation case demonstrating Commission's enforcement in financial markets.

AT.39824.conduct: Major truck manufacturers exchanged competitively sensitive information on gross list prices and the timing of introduction of emission technologies, coordinating surcharges for these technologies.
AT.39824.finding: Cartel involving price coordination and delayed introduction of emission technologies by DAF, Daimler, Iveco, MAN, Renault/Volvo and Scania.
AT.39824.court: DAF appeal (T-799/17) partially successful on fine calculation. Scania decision (AT.39824) challenged in T-799/17.
AT.39824.significance: Largest-ever EU cartel fine. Covered the entire EEA truck market over 14 years.

AT.40220.conduct: Qualcomm made significant payments to Apple on condition that Apple would exclusively use Qualcomm's LTE baseband chipsets in its iPhones and iPads.
AT.40220.finding: Exclusivity payments constituted an abuse of Qualcomm's dominant position on the LTE baseband chipset market under Article 102 TFEU.
AT.40220.court: Annulled by the General Court (T-235/18) in June 2022 due to procedural irregularities in the Commission's investigation. Commission appealed (C-566/22 P).
AT.40220.significance: Highlighted procedural requirements in Commission investigations. Rare full annulment of an abuse of dominance decision.

AT.40153.conduct: Amazon inserted clauses in its agreements with e-book publishers requiring them to notify Amazon of more favourable terms offered to competitors and to offer Amazon the same terms (most-favoured-nation clauses).
AT.40153.finding: Commission accepted commitments from Amazon to abandon its MFN clauses and refrain from enforcing them for 5 years.
AT.40153.court: No court challenge (commitments decision under Art. 9 Regulation 1/2003).
AT.40153.significance: First major digital platform MFN case. Led to wider scrutiny of platform parity clauses across the EU.

AT.40462.conduct: Amazon used non-public business data of independent sellers on its marketplace to inform its own retail decisions, and self-preferenced its own offers and those of sellers using its logistics service in the Buy Box.
AT.40462.finding: Commission accepted commitments from Amazon: (1) not to use non-public marketplace seller data for its retail business, (2) to provide equal treatment for Buy Box selection and Prime eligibility.
AT.40462.significance: Major platform self-preferencing case, closely linked to the DMA obligations later imposed on gatekeepers.

AT.40437.conduct: Apple required music streaming app developers (notably Spotify) to use Apple's in-app purchase system with a 30% commission, and prevented them from informing users about alternative subscription methods (anti-steering provisions).
AT.40437.finding: The Commission found that Apple's anti-steering provisions restricted music streaming app developers from informing iOS users about alternative purchasing options, constituting an abuse of dominance.
AT.40437.significance: First major App Store antitrust decision. Addressed anti-steering practices later also covered by the DMA.

AT.40652.conduct: Apple imposed restrictions on e-book and audiobook app developers, preventing them from informing users about cheaper purchasing options outside the App Store.
AT.40652.finding: Apple abused its dominant position by imposing anti-steering obligations on e-book/audiobook app developers, limiting consumer choice.
AT.40652.significance: Extended the Apple Music Streaming reasoning to e-books, reinforcing the anti-steering abuse theory.

AT.39258.conduct: Major airlines including Air France-KLM, British Airways, Cargolux, and others coordinated fuel surcharges, security surcharges, and the refusal to pay commission on surcharges for air cargo services.
AT.39258.finding: Global air cargo cartel involving coordination of various surcharge components on air cargo shipments worldwide.
AT.39258.court: Partially annulled and re-adopted multiple times. General Court (T-9/11 etc.) annulled the 2010 decision. Commission re-adopted in 2017.
AT.39258.significance: Major international cartel case illustrating the complexity of multi-jurisdictional enforcement.

AT.39226.conduct: Lundbeck, a pharmaceutical company, entered into agreements with generic drug manufacturers to delay their entry into the market for the antidepressant citalopram.
AT.39226.finding: Pay-for-delay agreements (reverse payment settlements) constituted restrictions of competition by object under Article 101 TFEU.
AT.39226.court: Upheld by the General Court (T-472/13) and confirmed by the ECJ (C-591/16 P) in its landmark 2021 ruling.
AT.39226.significance: Landmark ECJ ruling establishing that pay-for-delay agreements in the pharmaceutical sector constitute restrictions by object.

AT.39685.conduct: Servier, a pharmaceutical company, entered into patent settlement agreements with several generic companies to delay generic entry for its cardiovascular drug perindopril.
AT.39685.finding: Patent settlement agreements involving value transfers from the originator to generics in exchange for delayed market entry constituted restrictions of competition.
AT.39685.court: General Court (T-691/14) partially upheld. ECJ (C-176/19 P) in 2023 clarified the framework for assessing pay-for-delay agreements.
AT.39685.significance: Major ECJ ruling further refining the framework for pharmaceutical pay-for-delay agreements after Lundbeck.

AT.39612.conduct: Perindopril (Servier) - related case involving abuse of dominance through vexatious patent litigation and reverse payment settlements.
AT.39612.finding: Combined Article 101 and 102 infringement involving both anticompetitive agreements and abuse of the patent system.
AT.39612.significance: Demonstrated that patent-related conduct can simultaneously infringe both Articles 101 and 102 TFEU.

AT.39813.conduct: Baltic States shipping companies coordinated ferry services between Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania, fixing prices and sharing commercially sensitive information.
AT.39813.finding: Price-fixing and market-sharing cartel on Baltic ferry routes.
AT.39813.significance: Illustrative of Commission enforcement in transport sector.

AT.40049.conduct: MasterCard imposed inter-regional interchange fees on transactions where the card was issued outside the EEA but used at a merchant within the EEA.
AT.40049.finding: MasterCard's inter-regional multilateral interchange fees restricted competition by inflating costs for merchants.
AT.40049.significance: Major payment card interchange fee case building on prior MasterCard rulings.

AT.39398.conduct: Visa imposed multilateral interchange fees in the EEA that inflated costs for retailers and ultimately consumers.
AT.39398.finding: Commission accepted commitments from Visa to cap interchange fees for consumer credit card transactions at 0.3% of the transaction value.
AT.39398.significance: Together with MasterCard cases, led to the EU Interchange Fee Regulation capping fees across the EU.

AT.40670.conduct: Google is alleged to have leveraged its dominance in adtech intermediation services, favouring its own ad exchange and ad-buying tools while disadvantaging rivals.
AT.40670.finding: Pending investigation into Google's practices in the online advertising technology supply chain.
AT.40670.significance: Latest Google antitrust case targeting the digital advertising supply chain. Parallels the US DOJ adtech case.

AT.40983.conduct: Google is alleged to have engaged in practices related to AI and data that may reinforce its dominant position.
AT.40983.significance: First major EU competition case focused on AI practices and data advantages.

AT.41034.conduct: Meta is alleged to have excluded AI competitors from accessing WhatsApp's user base and data.
AT.41034.significance: Novel case addressing the intersection of messaging platforms, data access, and AI competition.

AT.40721.conduct: Microsoft is alleged to have abused its dominant position by tying Microsoft Teams with its Office 365 productivity suite.
AT.40721.finding: Pending. Commission accepted commitments from Microsoft in 2025 involving the unbundling of Teams from Office 365 in the EEA.
AT.40721.significance: Revived EU tying doctrine in the digital age. Led to Microsoft proactively unbundling Teams globally.

AT.39914.conduct: Major European and global banks manipulated the Euro Interest Rate Derivatives (EIRD) benchmark by coordinating submissions to the Euribor panel.
AT.39914.finding: Cartel involving manipulation of Euribor interest rate benchmark through collusion between traders at competing banks.
AT.39914.court: Multiple appeals. Several decisions partially annulled by General Court. HSBC decision annulled in part by ECJ (C-883/19 P).
AT.39914.significance: Major benchmark manipulation case. ECJ ruling in HSBC clarified the distinction between restriction by object and by effect.

AT.37990.conduct: Intel granted rebates to computer manufacturers (Dell, HP, NEC, Lenovo) conditional on them purchasing all or most of their x86 CPU needs from Intel, and paid Media Saturn to only sell Intel-based computers.
AT.37990.finding: Conditional rebates and naked restrictions constituted abuse of Intel's dominant position in the x86 CPU market.
AT.37990.court: Partially annulled by the General Court (T-286/09 RENV) in January 2022 following ECJ's landmark ruling (C-413/14 P) requiring effects-based analysis of loyalty rebates.
AT.37990.significance: The Intel saga (2009-2024) fundamentally reshaped EU law on loyalty rebates, requiring the Commission to prove likely anticompetitive effects rather than relying on form-based presumptions.

AT.39523.conduct: Slovak Telekom refused to provide competitors with access to its local loops (unbundled local loop access) and engaged in margin squeeze on wholesale broadband services.
AT.39523.finding: Refusal to supply and margin squeeze constituted abuse of dominant position under Article 102 TFEU.
AT.39523.court: General Court (T-851/14) and ECJ (C-165/19 P) upheld. ECJ ruling clarified the relationship between sector-specific regulation and competition law enforcement.
AT.39523.significance: Important ruling on the interface between telecom regulation and competition law, confirming that sector-specific obligations do not preclude Article 102 enforcement.
